coredata.dc:title: Multicolor carbon dots assembled polyvinyl alcohol with enhanced emission for white light-emitting diode
coredata.dc:creator: Kang, Yunyan
Li, Dongna
Dong, Riyue
Zhang, Haoran
Li, Wei
Zhang, Xuejie
Yang, Xian
Lei, Bingfu
coredata.dc:description: Red carbon dots (R-CDs), yellow-green carbon dots (Y-CDs) and blue carbon dots (B-CDs) were successfully synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and dopamine hydrochloride as raw materials by adjusting the pH of the reaction solvent. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra and optical properties of CDs show that these three CDs have similar types but different contents of functional groups, resulting in different fluorescence emission. R-CDs and Y-CDs compound with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to form bright R-CDs/PVA and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors, while B-CDs/PVA phosphors have weak fluorescence. Compared with R-CDs and Y-CDs solution, the fluorescence emission of the phosphors was significantly enhanced for the matrix inhibits the process of non-radiative transition. Based on the thermostability and broad emission peaks of CD-based phosphors, light-emitting diode (LED) was fabricated using R-CDs/PVA and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors as fluorescent material. The prepared LED exhibits white light with a color coordinate of (0.32, 0.33), correlated color temperature (CCT) of 6067 K and color rendering index (CRI) of 78.2. The environmental-friendly CD-based white light-emitting diode (WLED) was successfully fabricated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multicolor carbon dots
Polyvinyl alcohol
Fluorescence emission enhancement
White light-emitting diode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs
The formation of R-CDs/PVA phosphors and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) The TEM and HRTEM images of R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs, respectively.
The FT-IR spectra of R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs, respectively.
(a–c) The UV–vis spectra of R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs. (d–f) The fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs.

(a–b) The fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of R-CDs/PVA and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors. (c) The decay curves of R-CDs solution and R-CDs/PVA phosphors. (d) The decay curves of Y-CDs solution and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors.
(a) Photoluminescence spectra of the prepared WLED. (b) Color coordinates of CD-based WLED. (c) Photo of the working WLED.

head.ce:title: Multicolor carbon dots assembled polyvinyl alcohol with enhanced emission for white light-emitting diode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Horticulture, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, 510642, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Horticulture, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, 510642, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Maoming Branch, Guangdong Laboratory for Lingnan Modern Agriculture, Maoming, 525100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Laboratory for Lingnan Modern Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Maoming Branch, Guangdong Laboratory for Lingnan Modern Agriculture, Maoming 525100, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Horticulture, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, 510642, PR China. (B. Lei).
Corresponding author. College of Horticulture, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, PR China. (X. Yang).

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors have contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red carbon dots (R-CDs), yellow-green carbon dots (Y-CDs) and blue carbon dots (B-CDs) were successfully synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and dopamine hydrochloride as raw materials by adjusting the pH of the reaction solvent. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra and optical properties of CDs show that these three CDs have similar types but different contents of functional groups, resulting in different fluorescence emission. R-CDs and Y-CDs compound with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to form bright R-CDs/PVA and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors, while B-CDs/PVA phosphors have weak fluorescence. Compared with R-CDs and Y-CDs solution, the fluorescence emission of the phosphors was significantly enhanced for the matrix inhibits the process of non-radiative transition. Based on the thermostability and broad emission peaks of CD-based phosphors, light-emitting diode (LED) was fabricated using R-CDs/PVA and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors as fluorescent material. The prepared LED exhibits white light with a color coordinate of (0.32, 0.33), correlated color temperature (CCT) of 6067 K and color rendering index (CRI) of 78.2. The environmental-friendly CD-based white light-emitting diode (WLED) was successfully fabricated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a new kind of high-profile nanomaterial, mainly contain C, N and O elements. Compared with organic dyes, CDs have the characteristics of good light stability and low toxicity [1]. They are more environmentally friendly and more sustainable than rare-earth doped phosphors [2]. Compared with graphene and fullerene, CDs have unique color-tunable photoluminescence emission [3]. The superior performance of CDs makes it have broad application prospects in the fields of biological imaging, sensing, agriculture and optoelectronic devices [4–6]. Hydrothermal/solvothermal method is the most commonly used method for preparing CDs. Various fluorescent CDs have been successfully prepared yet. In the early stage of the development of CDs, more short-wavelength emitting CDs (such as blue light) were successfully prepared [7,8]. In recent years, more research focus on ultrabright red and near infrared (NIR) CDs and their corresponding applications. Moreover, the researchers also synthesized multi-color CDs at the same time by adjusting the reaction conditions or the precursors. For example, Li et al. used o-phenylenediamine as raw materials to prepare tri-color carbon dots (Tri-CDs) by one-step hydrothermal synthesis and silica gel column chromatography separation [9]. O-phenylenediamine and citric acid as raw materials, by changing the reaction temperature, the amount of precursors and the pH of the solvent, Lu's team successfully synthesized multi-color luminescent CDs (blue to red) [10]. Wang et al. prepared CDs by oil bath heating, and synthesized CDs dispersed in different solvents have different fluorescence (from green to red) after precipitation and centrifugation [11]. The method of synthesizing multi-color CDs usually requires the assistance of complex silica gel column chromatography, and the synthesis yield of the obtained CDs is very low. Hence, finding strategies to prepare polychromatic CDs with simpler methods and precursors is significant.

According to statistics from the US Department of Energy, 15% of the world's energy is used for lighting. It is estimated that by 2030, population growth and accelerated urbanization will lead to a continued increase in the energy consumption for lighting [2]. As a new type of lighting device, the light-emitting diode (LED) has the advantages of smaller size, long fluorescence lifetime, low pollution, high efficiency and energy saving. Compared with compact fluorescent lamps (CFL), LED lighting devices can reduce energy demand and greenhouse gas emissions by 41–50% [12]. In recent years, the research and application of LED lights have attracted the attention of many scientists, and lots of outstanding results have appeared in the public view [13–16]. There are many types of fluorescent materials that can be used in LEDs, including: fluorescent CDs, quantum dots, perovskite luminescent materials, organic luminescent materials, metal organic framework materials and traditional phosphors [17,18]. Among them, the application of traditional phosphors in LEDs has reached the level of commercialization. The most important light-emitting device in the field of lighting is WLED. Humans have evolved over a long period of time to adapt to sunlight. White LED is a kind of light-emitting devices suitable for human daily lighting according to human habits. Among them, yellow light YAG: Ce3+ (Y3Al5O12: Ce3+) phosphor is a famous rare-earth doped phosphor used in WLED lamps, and it has been widely used in daily lighting nowadays. In addition, there are endless applications of various fluorescent materials in WLEDs. Yang et al. prepared ZnS-coated Zn–In–S quantum dots co-doped with Cu and Mn with a QY of 75% [19]. The quantum dots have white light emission with a color coordinate of (0.332, 0394) and a color rendering index (CRI) of 71. Qiu et al. prepared blue, green and red luminescent perovskite luminescent materials CsPbCl1.8Br1.2, CsPbBr3, CsPbBr1.2I1.8 nanocrystals and packaged them into WLEDs by in-situ synthesis [20]. Liu et al. obtained white light-emitting materials by coating a variety of fluorescent dyes with a metal organic frame, and finally obtained a WLED with a color coordinate of (0.32, 0.34) [21]. In addition, the research and application of CDs in WLEDs has also attracted much attention. Xu et al. used p-phenylenediamine and glycine as precursors to prepare red, green, and blue CDs by solvothermal method [22]. The prepared CDs compounded polyvinylpyrrolidone were encapsulated to obtain WLED. It is particularly important to discover luminescent materials that can be applied to WLEDs. How to obtain luminescent materials that can more perfect the light color quality of light-emitting devices, make the light environment more comfortable and healthier, is an important goal of research on WLEDs.

Compared with perovskites, semiconductor quantum dots and traditional phosphors, CD-based LEDs are more environmentally friendly, and are also a research area that has attracted much attention in recent years. However, under normal circumstances, CDs need to be combined with other substrates to form solid phosphors in order to be better applied to LEDs, such as starch, silicon dioxide, polyvinyl alcohol, cellulose, etc., to use the role of the matrix to overcome their aggregation [23]. For example, Wang et al. used polymethyl methacrylate as a matrix and compounded NIR CDs, and then the CD-based materials were used for white light-emitting diode (WLED) [24]. Chen et al. combined CDs with polyvinylpyrrolidone to form a thin film with blue to red fluorescence, thereby preparing WLED [25]. Generally, blue, green, and red CDs are commonly used for CD-based fluorescent materials for WLED. Generally, WLED can be configured with three primary colors in proportion to form white light under ultraviolet excitation. For example, Qu et al. (2017) synthesized multi-color fluorescent CDs as light-conversion materials to obtain brighter multi-color solid-state luminescent materials, and applied them in WLED [26]. White light emission can also be achieved by mixing two complementary light colors, such as blue and yellow. Song et al. assembled blue and orange CDs on the surface of polystyrene nanoparticles, and then encapsulated WLEDs with high CRI [24]. In addition, single-component CDs can also give white light. Wang et al. prepared a carbon dot with a QY of up to 41% with dual emission of blue fluorescence and yellow-green phosphorescence, and then encapsulated the luminescent material into an ultraviolet chip to obtain a WLED [27].

In this work, dopamine hydrochloride and o-phenylenediamine are used as raw materials. Through adjusting the pH of the reaction solution, red carbon dots (R-CDs), yellow-green carbon dots (Y-CDs), and blue carbon dots (B-CDs) are obtained by a one-step hydrothermal method. The synthesis yields of R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs are approximately 25%, 5% and 5%. The QY of R-CDs, Y-CDs, and B-CDs are 28%, 3%, and 2%, respectively. YCDs and PVA are compounded to obtain yellow-green fluorescent Y-CDs/PVA phosphors, and R-CDs are compounded with cellulose powder and PVA to obtain red light R-CDs/cellulose phosphors and R-CDs/PVA phosphors, respectively. After the CDs solution is compounded with the PVA matrix, the fluorescence emission is significantly enhanced. The QY of the red R-CDs/PVA phosphor is 47%, and the QY of the yellow-green Y-CDs/PVA phosphor is 36%. Using yellow fluorescent YCDs/PVA phosphors and red fluorescent R-CDs/PVA phosphors as luminescent materials, packaged in a 450 nm blue-emission chip, the color coordinates of obtained WLED is (0.32, 0.33), CCT is 6067 K, and the CRI is 78.2.

O-phenylenediamine was purchased from Aladdin. Dopamine hydrochloride and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Ethanol and NaOH purchased from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. Concentrated hydrochloric acid was purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory. The 450 nm blue-emission chip was purchased from Huangzhi Lighting Accumulation Co., Ltd.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained from a JEOL JEM-2100 F electron microscope. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were taken with a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer. The Raman spectra were recorded by Raman spectroscopy (Thermo DXR2xi). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) were carried out with Brooker (Vertex 70) Fourier infrared spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) patterns were recorded by X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (Thermo ESCALAB 250XI). Fluorescence spectra were recorded with an F-700 Hitachi fluorescence spectrofluorometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a JASCO v-570 spectrofluorometer. The decay lifetimes and quantum yield (QY) were measured by Edinburgh FLS1000 spectrofluorometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 184 mg dopamine hydrochloride and 216 mg o-phenylenediamine were added into 20 mL of deionized water, and the pH of the solution was adjusted below 0.5 with HCl. Then the mixed solution was transferred to 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave after 10 min ultrasonic treatment. After that, Teflon reactor was moved to a 180 °C oven for 6 h. The obtained solution that was naturally cooled to room temperature was directly dialyzed in a 3500 Da dialysis bag for one day and then collect sediments which are R-CDs aggregates from the dialysis bag. R-CDs powder can be easily dispersed in the ethanol for further application.

As for Y-CDs, the preparation method of them is the same as those of R-CDs except that no pH adjustment is required (the pH of solvent is 6.5). And B-CDs can be synthesized from the same precursors as R-CDs and Y-CDs by adjusting the pH of the solution to 11 by NaOH. The B-CDs solution was obtained directly after hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 6 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The formation of R-CDs/PVA phosphors and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors
ce:section.ce:para: R-CDs and Y-CDs powders were dissolved in ethanol to obtain R-CDs solution and Y-CDs solution, respectively. The R-CDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) is mixed with PVA (10 mL R-CDs solution is compounded with 1 g matrix), and then the mixed solution is placed at room temperature and stirred for 6 h. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove the supernatant. Then the precipitate was washed twice with ethanol, and the resulting precipitate was placed in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C to dry for 1 day to obtain R-CDs/PVA phosphors. Similarly, Y-CDs/PVA phosphor was obtained in the same way.

The chips that emitted 450 nm light at the operation voltage of 3.0 V were the excitation light source of the LED lamp. R-CDs/PVA phosphors and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors are mixed at a ratio of 3:1, and the mixture was thoroughly stirred until homogeneous. Then the mixed colloid was dropped onto the 450-nm chip by dispensing, and it is naturally left to be air-dried for several hours. Finally, the lid of the LED lamp bead is covered.

R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs were prepared by one step hydrothermal synthesis through adjusting the pH of the solution from dopamine hydrochloride and o-phenylenediamine. From Fig. 1 (a-c), the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs show that they are spherical. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) show that these three CDs have obvious lattices fringes with an interplanar of 0.21 nm which consistent with the (100) diffraction facets of graphitic [28]. Besides, the average diameters of R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs are 2.40 nm, 2.87 and 2.33 nm, respectively. Moreover, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Figure S1) reveals that the crystallization degree decreases from R-CDs to B-CDs. R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs exhibit a peak at 21.5°, signifying the amorphous nature of them. A micro peak at approximately 44.6° corresponds to the (100) facet with an interlayer spacing of 0.21 nm, which is consistent with TEM images [29]. The XRD pattern of R-CDs have an apparent peak at 25.2°, which is attributed to the (002) facet with an interlayer spacing of 0.34 nm. Figure S2 shows the Raman spectra of R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs [30]. From the Raman spectra, the intensity ratios of the crystalline G band at 1540 cm−1 and disordered D band at 1370 cm−1 (IG/ID) are 1.04, 1.08 and 1.14 from R-CDs to B-CDs, respectively, demonstrating that R-CDs have more amorphous structures, while the graphitization degree of B-CDs strengthens [31]. The XRD patterns and Raman spectra show opposite results, which is similar to the previous work [32]. As we all know, the structure of CDs and the corresponding CDs powders are complex. As for these two characterizations, XRD can accurately determine the crystal structure inside the material, while the Raman spectra reflect the vibration information of functional groups on the surface of the materials. Hence, the XRD patterns can better reflect the crystallization degree of CDs. R-CDs have higher crystallization degree.

The chemical structures of R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs were further analyzed through FT-IR and XPS spectra. FT-IR spectra of these three CDs are showed in Fig. 2 . It reveals that the vibrations of them are quite similar, which means that their surface chemical structures are similar. The reason of these phenomenon is that the precursors for the preparation of these three kinds of CDs are the same. The broad peak at 3500-3200 cm−1 belongs to O–H and N–H stretching vibration. The sharp absorption peaks at 2925 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of C–H, and the absorption peaks at 1620 and 1470 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of CC/CN and the bending vibration of N–H, respectively. The absorption peak of 1250–1380 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the C–N bond. The absorption peaks at 1130 cm−1 belong to C–O stretching vibration respectively. The absorption peak at 750 cm−1 is attributed to out-of-plane bending vibration of C–H [33,34]. However, the degree of vibration of these three CDs, which is related to the content of the corresponding bonds, is different. Moreover, the vibration of Y-CDs at 2925 cm−1 is stronger than R-CDs and B-CDs, indicating that the chemical structures are slightly different. In Figure S3a, the XPS spectrum shown that R-CDs mainly consist of C, N, O elements, and the atomic ratios are 79.36%, 14.20% and 6.44%. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Figure S3b) show four peaks at 284.8, 286.0, 286.8 and 288.4 eV, corresponding to the C–C/CC, C–N, C–O and CN bonds respectively. In Figure S3c, the N 1s spectrum of R-CDs was resolved into three peaks with a peak value of 398.8, 400.4 and 402.1 eV, corresponding to pyridine N, pyrrolic N and graphite N, respectively. The two peaks at 530.3 and 531.2 eV of the O 1s spectrum indicate the presence of CO and C–OH/C–O–C bonds of R-CDs [35,36]. Figure S4 shows the XPS spectra of Y-CDs, which reveals that Y-CDs contains three elements, including C, N and O elements, with the content of 77.35%, 2.04% and 20.61%, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Figure S4b) reveals the existence of C–C/CC (284.8 eV) and C–O (286.4eV). The N 1s spectrum in Figure S4c can be fitted into two peaks at 399.2 and 400.5 eV, corresponding to the presence of pyridine N and pyrrole N. In Figure S2d, O 1s of Y-CDs contains two peaks, belonging to C–OH/C–O–C (532.2 eV) and C–O (533.85 eV) respectively. Besides, Figure S5 is the XPS spectra of B-CDs. B-CDs mainly have C, N and O elements, with the atomic ratio of 76.86%, 6.96% and 16.18% according to Figure S5a, respectively. In Figure S5b, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.8, 286.3 and 288.0 eV, ascribed to C–C/CC, C–O and CN/CO, respectively. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Figure S5c) could be fitted into two peaks, corresponding to pyridine N (399.1eV) and pyrrole N (400.5eV), respectively. The three peaks of 530.7, 531.9 and 533 eV in the O 1s high-resolution spectrum (Figure S5d) indicate the presence of CO, C–OH/C–O–C and C–O, respectively. The results of XPS data manifest that types of the functional group of three CDs are similar in a mass, while the relative content of them are different. The above characterization indicates that the surfaces of R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs are rich in a variety of functional groups, including C–C, CC, CN, C–O, O–H, N–H and other chemical bonds.

The optical properties of R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs were further explored. Fig. 3 (a-c) are the UV–vis spectra of R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs. Fig. 3a shows that R-CDs has intense absorption at 285 nm which assigned to π→π* transition of the aromatic CC bond, while the absorption at 450–600 nm is due to the n →π* transition of the CO and CN bonds. The UV–vis spectrum of Y-CDs (Fig. 3b) exhibits two absorption band at the range of 230–330 nm and 330–500 nm, corresponding to the π→π* transition of the aromatic CC bond and the n→π* transition of the CN bond [37]. The absorption of B-CDs is mainly located before 400 nm, which is closely related to the π→π* transition of the CC bond, which is consistent with most reported blue CDs [38]. According to Fig. 3 (d-f), R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs emit red, yellow green and blue light, respectively. Fig. 3 (d-f) are the fluorescence emission spectra and excitation spectra of R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs. From Fig. 3d, the optimal excitation wavelength of R-CDs is 540 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength is 600 nm. With the change of excitation wavelength, the position of emission spectrum does not shift, indicating the excitation independence characteristic of R-CDs. In addition, the QY of R-CDs in ethanol is 28%. In Fig. 3e, Y-CDs can be excited by ultraviolet light and blue light. The optimal emission wavelength of Y-CDs is 540 nm, with yellow-green fluorescence, and the QY is 3%. They also have excitation independence property. In Fig. 3f, the fluorescence spectrum of B-CDs with QY of 2% show that the optimal excitation wavelength is 435 nm and the optimal emission is 490 nm. By adjusting the pH of the solvent，CDs with different fluorescence were synthesized using the same raw material, indicating that the pH of the solution plays an important role in the Formation of CDs. Combining the FT-IR and XPS spectra, the result can be concluded that the types and contents of functional groups are related to the fluorescence of CDs.

CDs dispersed in solvents have good luminescence. In general, after CDs are dried into a solid form, fluorescence quenching will occur because of aggregation. In order to forbid the fluorescence quenching of CDs, CDs are compound with the matrix to make them disperse into matrix. Similarly, the prepared R-CDs, Y-CDs and B-CDs have red fluorescence, yellow-green fluorescence and blue fluorescence in the solution, respectively, but the CDs in solid state has no fluorescence. Hence, the CDs was combined with PVA to form R-CDs/PVA phosphors and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors. However, when B-CDs compound with matrix such as PVA, cellulose powder, starch and so on, the fluorescence of mixture is weak. Hence, B-CDs are not considered for further application. As shown in Fig. 4 (a-b), the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs/PVA phosphors and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors are similar to those of R-CDs and Y-CDs, respectively, indicating that the fluorescence of CD-based phosphors origins from CDs. The excitation of the Y-CDs/PVA phosphors is exactly matched with the emission of the R-CDs/PVA phosphors. Besides, the QYs of R-CDs/PVA phosphors and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors are 47% and 36%, respectively. Compared with QY of R-CDs (28%) and Y-CDs (3%) solution, the fluorescence emission of the phosphors is significantly enhanced, increasing by 19% and 33%. When CDs are compounded with other matrixes, the optical properties pf the obtain CD-based phosphors are lower. As shown in Figure S6, compound of CDs and starch has no fluorescence, while the quantum yield of the phosphors that R-CDs or Y-CDs combine with mesoporous silica or cellulose, respectively, are lower than that of R-CDs/PVA and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors.

R-CDs and Y-CDs assembled with PVA have brighter fluorescence. In order to further explore the luminescence dynamics of R-CDs/PVA phosphors and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors. As shown in Fig. 4c-d, the lifetimes of CDs and corresponding phosphors are recorded. In Fig. 4c, the decay curves of R-CDs solution and R-CDs/PVA phosphors can be fitted with a single-exponential function, and the average lifetimes of R-CDs solution and R-CDs/PVA phosphors are 2.23 and 2.76 nm, respectively. And the decay curves of Y-CDs solution and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors can be fitted with a single exponential. The average lifetime of R-CDs solution is 2.23 ns, and the average lifetime of R-CDs/PVA is 2.76 ns. Fig. 4d shows the lifetime decay curves of Y-CDs solution and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors. They can be fitted with double exponential function, with the average lifetimes of 4.05 and 4.65 ns, respectively. The average lifetime (τ), QY, radiation attenuation (k
r ) and non-radiation attenuation (k
nr ) of fluorescent materials are interrelated, and the relationship is as follows:
(1)
1 τ
=
k r
+
k
n r
(2)
QY =
k r
k r
+
k
n r

According to Formula (1) & (2), combing to τ and QY, k
r and k
nr of R-CDs, R-CDs/PVA phosphors, Y-CDs, Y-CDs/PVA phosphors are calculated, and the results are showed in Table S1. The lifetime of R-CDs/PVA phosphors is longer than that of R-CDs solution. According to reports, the decay of fluorescence lifetime is directly related to the environment in which the fluorescent material is located [39]. When R-CDs are dispersed in ethanol, k
r is 12.6 × 107 s−1. After R-CDs dispersed in PVA, k
r increases to 16.9 × 107 s−1, indicating longer radiative decay processes. At the same time, k
nr decreased from 3.24 × 108 s−1 to 1.91 × 108 s−1, which means that when R-CDs are dispersed in the matrix, the matrix inhibits the process of non-radiative transition and reduces the energy loss, thus making R-CDs have a higher QY in the solid state. Besides, the fluorescence emission of Y-CDs in the matrix is also significantly enhanced. By calculation, from Y-CDs solution to Y-CDs/PVA phosphors, k
r increases from 12.6 × 107 s−1 to 7.92 × 107 s−1, which proves that the radiation transition process becomes longer. In the same times, k
nr decreases from 2.43 × 108 s−1 to 1.71 × 108 s−1, which proves that the non-radiative transition is reduced and the energy loss is lower, leading to the enhancement of fluorescence emission of Y-CDs/PVA phosphors. Therefore, PVA is an ideal matrix and effective dispersion for the enhancement of the QY of R-CDs and Y-CDs.

Based on the unique fluorescence of R-CDs/PVA phosphors and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors, they are served as fluorescent material for the fabrication LEDs. The thermostability is the basic character of the Phosphors for LEDs. The fluorescence changes of R-CDs/PVA phosphors and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors with the temperature going up are showed in Figure S7 (a-b). When the temperature reaches a fixed value after increasing the temperature, the temperature is kept unchanged for 5 min, and then the fluorescence spectrum of the phosphors will be measured. As the temperature increases, the fluorescence intensity of the phosphors decreases. When the temperature rises to 150 °C, the fluorescence emission intensity of R-CDs/PVA phosphors and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors decrease by 22% and 25%, respectively. Besides, the fluorescence intensities of R-CDs/PVA phosphors and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors are almost constant at 50 °C for several hours, while the intensities of them decrease by nearly 20% at 150 °C for 30 min. The stability of CD-based phosphors is lower than that of rare-earth doped phosphors. Hence, Improving the stability of CD-based phosphors is an important research direction.

The R-CDs/PVA phosphor Y-CDs/PVA is fabricated into a 450 nm blue-emission chip in a ratio of 1:3. The emission spectrum of the LED is shown in Fig. 5 a, covering the entire spectrum of visible light. The color coordinate of the prepared LED is showed in Fig. 5b. The emission of LED is located in the white light area with color coordinate of (0.32, 0.33). The corresponding CCT of this CD-based WLED is 6067 K, and the CRI is 78.2. Fig. 5c is the photo of the working WLED fabricated with R-CDs/PVA and Y-CDs/PVA phosphor and blue-emission chip. In summary, CD-based phosphors can be fabricated to WLED.

A variety of fluorescent CDs were successfully prepared by controlling the pH of the reaction solvent. The content of functional groups of the prepared CDs leads to different fluorescence emission of CDs. R-CDs and Y-CDs dispersed in the PVA matrix to form solid state luminescent materials, avoiding the quenching caused by aggregation. The fluorescence emission of R-CDs/PVA and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors was obviously enhanced due to the enhancement of radiative transition process and the decline of non-radiative transition process. R-CDs/PVA and Y-CDs/PVA phosphors are mixed to fabricate WLED with environmentally-friendly and sustainable luminous materials. This study has a guiding significance for the application of CD-based LED.

Yunyan Kang and Dongna Li provided the synthesis method for the nanomaterials, carried out the experimental part, analyzed the data and wrote the Original Draft. Riyue Dong and Haoran Zhang provided useful suggestions and discussion concerning this manuscript. Wei Li helped to synthesize the samples. Xuejie Zhang performed the measurements and data analysis. Xian Yang and Bingfu Lei provided the idea for this work and acted and revised the pictures and grammar in the manuscript as the project administrator.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Guangdong Provincial Special Fund for Modern Agriculture Industry Technology Innovation Teams (No. 2022KJ122 and 2023KJ122), the Independent Research and Development Projects of Maoming Laboratory (No. 2021ZZ004), the National Natural Science Foundations of China (No. 52102042), the Key Realm R&D Program of Guangdong Province (No. 2021B0707010003), the Guangzhou Science & Technology Project (Nos. 202007020005, 202103000059), the Guangdong Provincial Science and Technology Project (Nos. 2021A0505050006, 2022A1515010229), and the Project of GDUPS (2018) for Prof. Bingfu LEI.
